11: The historical context of the Exodus is debated: the most common evangelical dating places the Exodus ca. 1446 BCE (the 'early date,' based on 1 Kings 6:1's 480 years from Exodus to Solomon's temple ca. 966 BCE), during Thutmose III's reign. The 'late date' (ca. 1270-1250 BCE, during Ramesses II's reign) is favored by many archaeologists based on the cities Pithom and Ramesses mentioned in Exodus 1:11. The Merneptah Stele (ca. 1208 BCE) mentions Israel as a people already in Canaan, providing a terminus ad quem. The question remains open; the theological significance of the Exodus is independent of the exact date.
1: The opening phrase — ve-eleh shemot, 'and these are the names' — connects Exodus directly to Genesis (Genesis 46:8 opens the same list with ve-eleh shemot). The 'and' signals narrative continuity, not a new story. The sons of Israel who came to Egypt are the same individuals whose stories ended in Genesis; Exodus begins where Genesis left off. The list serves as a genealogical bridge, reminding the reader that what follows is not a new divine initiative but the continuation of the covenant made with the patriarchs.
2: The six sons of Leah named first (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun), then Rachel's sons (Benjamin — Joseph is not listed because he was already in Egypt, cf. Exodus 1:5), then the sons of the concubines (Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher). The listing order approximates Genesis 35:23-26 but adapts it. This is not a complete genealogy (wives and daughters are not listed) but a patriarchal inventory — the seed of the nation that will be formed at Sinai.
3: Issachar, Zebulun — two of Leah's sons. The tribal names in the list anticipate the twelve-tribe structure that will be organized at Sinai and assigned territories in Canaan. The reader of the completed Torah knows each name carries the weight of a whole tribe's history; the text names them individually before the mass of 'the people' obscures the personal.
4: Benjamin (son of Rachel, born in Canaan in Genesis 35:18) and the four sons of the concubines close the list. The ordering of the twelve reflects the complex family hierarchy of Jacob's household — a social structure of competing matrilineal claims that will later surface in tribal rivalries throughout the Deuteronomistic history.
5: Seventy persons descended from Jacob — a formulaic total (see also Genesis 46:27 and Deuteronomy 10:22 which give the same number, though with slightly different counting methods). Seventy is a significant round number in ANE thought, representing completeness (70 elders of Israel in Ex Exodus 24:1, 70 nations in the Table of Nations Gen 10). The family of Jacob in Egypt is a complete, bounded unit — a nation-in-seed, not yet a nation in fact.
6: Joseph and all his brothers and all that generation died — the bridge between the patriarchal era and the slavery era is removed. The connection to the Egyptian court that protected Israel (Joseph's role in Gen 41-50) no longer operates. This death notice marks the end of an era and sets the conditions for the persecution to follow: without a Joseph in the palace, there is no advocate for the Hebrews.
7: The multiplication of Israel in Egypt is described with six verbs of growth — language that echoes the creation mandate of Genesis 1:28 (paru urvu = be fruitful and multiply). The Israelites in Goshen live out the Adamic commission: fruitful, multiplying, filling the land. Historically, the Goshen region (eastern Nile delta, modern Wadi Tumilat) was fertile agricultural land suitable for pastoralists, and Egyptian records document Semitic populations in this region during the New Kingdom period.
8: A new king who did not know Joseph — the phrase asher lo-yada et-Yosef signals either a dynasty change or a deliberate political distancing from the previous administration. The most likely historical scenario: the Hyksos (Semitic rulers of northern Egypt, ca. 1650-1550 BC) were expelled by the native Egyptian 18th Dynasty, which had reason to view the Semitic population of Goshen with suspicion. Alternatively, under the late-date theory, Ramesses II (ca. 1279-1213 BC) initiated the large building projects at Pi-Ramesse. Either scenario produces a Pharaoh hostile to the Israelite presence.
9: Pharaoh frames the Israelites as a demographic security threat: 'more numerous and mightier than we.' Whether or not this was statistically true, the perception drove policy. Ancient Near Eastern rulers regularly used population politics — relocating, enslaving, or eliminating groups perceived as threats. The fear articulated in Exodus 1:10 (that the Israelites might join an enemy and 'go up out of the land') reflects the standard anxiety of a state with a large, unassimilated ethnic minority near its northern border.
10: 'Let us deal shrewdly with them' — the word for dealing shrewdly (nitchakemah) is ironic: human wisdom applied against the covenant people will ultimately prove foolish (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:27). Pharaoh's strategy is to suppress demographic growth through forced labor. The phrase 'go up out of the land' anticipates the exodus itself — Pharaoh's fear will become precisely the reality he was trying to prevent. Egyptian records document the use of Asiatic foreigners as state labor, particularly in large construction projects.
12: The Egyptians are in dread of the Israelites — vayaqutzu mipnei benei Yisrael, they felt sick/loathing at the sight of them. The persecution paradox is stated starkly: the more they were afflicted, the more they multiplied. This counterintuitive dynamic recurs throughout biblical history (Acts 8:1-4: persecution of the early church scatters and thereby spreads it). The Egyptian oppression cannot suppress what God is doing — the divine blessing on the seed of Abraham is not extinguishable by human force.
13: The intensification of labor — abodah parach, crushing/harsh labor. This exact phrase later appears as the legal norm in Leviticus 25:43, 46, 53 where Israel is commanded not to rule over fellow Israelites with crushing labor. The Exodus experience becomes the ethical foundation of Israel's own labor law: you know what it is to be worked to breaking point; do not do this to others.
14: Mortar, brick, and all field labor — mudbrick production was the dominant labor-intensive construction material in ancient Egypt. Workers mixed Nile mud with straw (a binder that prevented cracking) and shaped it in wooden molds. Bricks were sun-dried rather than kiln-fired. The Anastasi papyri from the 19th Dynasty describe foreign workers making bricks, with overseers recording daily production quotas. The 'bitter' labor (vayemareru) uses the same root as Marah (the bitter water, Ex Exodus 15:23) and the bitter herbs of the Passover meal — the Exodus liturgy encodes this bitterness into the annual memorial feast.
15: Shiphrah and Puah — both are Semitic names, attested in the historical record. An Egyptian slave list from ca. 1750 BC (Brooklyn Papyrus) contains the name Shiphrah among Semitic servants in Egypt. This historical grounding is significant: the narrative names real women who performed a real act of civil disobedience. Their names are preserved while Pharaoh — the most powerful figure on earth — remains nameless throughout the Exodus narrative. The one who tries to destroy Israel is unnamed; the women who preserved Israel are named.
16: The birthstool (obnayim) was the delivery stone or stool on which a woman in labor knelt or sat during childbirth. Egyptian art and texts depict women giving birth in a kneeling or squatting position supported by two stones or a birth chair. The midwives' role was to attend the birth and receive the infant. Pharaoh's instruction — kill the boys at the moment of birth — required the midwives to be agents of infanticide in the most vulnerable moment of human life, exploiting their position of trust.
17: The midwives feared God (vayyire'u hamyaledot et-ha'Elohim) — the first recorded act of principled civil disobedience in the Bible. Their theological conviction — fear of God superseding fear of the state — is the basis for their refusal. This pattern (God over Pharaoh, God over Nebuchadnezzar, God over the Sanhedrin) runs through the entire biblical narrative and into the NT (Acts 5:29: 'We must obey God rather than human beings'). The midwives did not theorize their disobedience; they simply refused to do what was wrong.
18: Pharaoh summons the midwives to account — the most powerful man in the ancient world interrogating two women about why they did not carry out his genocidal decree. The power asymmetry is enormous; the narrative's sympathies are entirely with the midwives. Their answer (Exodus 1:19) — 'the Hebrew women are vigorous and give birth before the midwife arrives' — may be a tactful deflection rather than a literal claim, though it exploits a real characteristic of birth timing.
19: The characterization of Hebrew women as chayyot — 'lively/vigorous' (the word also means animal-like in vitality, full of life) — is the midwives' cover story. Whether this is an outright lie or a strategic half-truth the narrative leaves ambiguous. Ancient rabbinic discussion engaged this question seriously, debating whether the midwives' deception was justified. The narrative evaluates them by their outcome (God blessed them, Exodus 1:20) rather than analyzing the ethics of their statement — a pragmatic rather than prescriptive response to the moral complexity of unjust authority.
20: God blessed the midwives and the people multiplied — the two effects of the midwives' faithful defiance. Divine blessing (vayetev Elohim lamyaledot) answers their act of courage with concrete flourishing. The multiplication of Israel is directly attributed to the midwives' refusal. Historical and theological agency intersect here: God's sovereign plan to increase Israel uses the free moral choice of two women to achieve what no divine intervention at the moment required. The ordinary courage of ordinary people becomes the mechanism of divine action.
21: God gave the midwives households (battim) — families of their own. The midwives who preserved the lives of Israel's children received children (and family lines) of their own. The reward is proportionate: they gave life; they receive life. Some ancient interpreters identified Shiphrah and Puah with Jochebed and Miriam (or Zipporah), making them the mothers or family of Moses himself — an attractive but unverifiable connection.
22: The decree extended to all Egyptians — every newborn Hebrew boy is to be thrown into the Nile. The genocide order is nationalized: not just the midwives but all Egyptian subjects are now enlisted as agents of destruction. The Nile — Egypt's source of life, the god Hapy's domain, the agricultural foundation of the civilization — becomes the instrument of infanticide. The irony runs deep: the river that gives Egypt its life is commanded to take Israel's life; but the same river will preserve Moses (Exodus 2) and ultimately become the first plague-target (Ex Exodus 7:20), turning life-giving water to blood.
